grades.note: Admission-reform mechanisms (alternative assessment formats, SES proxies) are untested in the Hungarian context.

An SES-proxy or quota mechanism risks becoming a new, opaque gatekeeping tool that simply relocates coaching advantage to portfolio or interview preparation instead of genuinely widening access for disadvantaged pupils.

A less coaching-sensitive admission mechanism could widen the effective talent pool available to employers and higher education, since the current exam mainly rewards coachability rather than aptitude.

Because admission-mechanism reform stays within existing ministerial authority and can be piloted, adjusted, or reversed within a single electoral cycle, it carries lower political and legal entrenchment risk than structural reform.

A state-sector cap on selective places risks being neutralised or even worsening equity if non-state (church, foundation, private) maintainers expand to absorb displaced demand, since no enforceable mechanism currently prevents this offsetting.

The phase-down of selective places must be paired with a statutory, pre-secured funding and staffing floor for general schools receiving displaced and retained pupils, not resources promised only after each cap reduction.

A gradual, multi-year, monitored phase-down is preferable to abrupt structural abolition because it gives families, employers, and schools time to adjust planning horizons, provided a segregation-tracking dashboard and lead-time guarantees exist.
Shrinking gimnazium cohort caps reduces teaching posts for gimnazium staff with no stated transition or retraining plan for the affected teachers.
Hungary faces a sustained teacher shortage and an ageing teacher workforce, concentrated in disadvantaged regions and in specific subject areas.
Free school choice plus early selective tracks produces strong between-school sorting already at the primary stage in cities.
Cross-country difference-in-differences evidence indicates earlier between-school tracking increases inequality of outcomes.
Poland's 1999 reform postponed tracking to age 15 (gimnazjum); PISA scores rose substantially by 2012, with analyses attributing this to the reform. However, the reform was reversed in 2016/19.

Portuguese TIMSS-2023 data show a decline in the share of low-SES high-performing pupils (21% to 16%, 2015-2024) despite 16 years of TEIP-style compensatory support, raising doubt that compensatory investment offsets the effects of early selection once cohorts are sorted.

The scenario itself identifies TEIP-style targeted funding as a likely first-line cut during fiscal consolidation, meaning the compensatory package's central benefit-delivery mechanism is politically and fiscally fragile absent a statutory funding floor.
Portugal improved PISA results 2006-2015 without changing selection age, via curriculum standards, school clustering, targeted support, and early-leaving reduction programs (TEIP).

S4 avoids the statutory entrenchment, cross-party consensus precondition, and reversal risk that structural reform (S2, S3) requires, making it more fiscally and politically durable within a single electoral cycle.
Hungary faces a sustained teacher shortage and an ageing teacher workforce, concentrated in disadvantaged regions and in specific subject areas.
Since 2013 most state schools are run by state school district centres (tankerulet); churches and foundations maintain a distinct legal status and autonomy over admissions and pedagogy.

Without a statutory workload floor and matching staffing investment, teachers in general schools will absorb larger, more heterogeneous classes as displaced and retained pupils arrive, amounting to a de facto phase-out of public teaching capacity.
